exercises.title: Majority vote beats one voter
exercises.prompt: Show numerically that majority voting over many independent weak classifiers (each 60% accurate) beats any single one. Compute the ensemble accuracy for an odd number of voters.
exercises.hint: Ensemble is correct when more than half the independent voters are correct — a binomial tail probability.

exercises.title: Bagging reduces variance
exercises.prompt: Build a bagging ensemble: train several models on bootstrap resamples and average their predictions. Demonstrate that the averaged prediction has lower variance than a single model. Explain why bias is unchanged.
exercises.hint: Bootstrap = sample-with-replacement of the training set. Average the predictions; variance of the mean of m decorrelated estimators is ~1/m of one.
exercises.solution:
```python
import random
def bootstrap(data):
    return [random.choice(data) for _ in range(len(data))]
# each base model trained on its own bootstrap sample; predict() averaged
# variance of the ensemble mean of m near-independent models ~ var_single/m
```

Each model sees a different bootstrap sample, so their errors are partly **decorrelated**. Averaging m such predictions cuts variance roughly by a factor up to m (limited by residual correlation) — while leaving **bias unchanged**, because each model is unbiased for the same target and the average of unbiased estimators is still unbiased. That's exactly why random forests (bagged trees + feature randomness) turn high-variance deep trees into a stable, accurate model.

exercises.title: Bagging vs boosting vs stacking
exercises.prompt: For each of bagging, boosting, and stacking: explain how it combines models and which error component (bias or variance) it primarily targets. Why does boosting reduce bias where bagging can't?
exercises.hint: Bagging = parallel + average (variance). Boosting = sequential, each model fixes the last's errors (bias). Stacking = a meta-model learns to combine base models.
exercises.solution:
- **Bagging** trains many models *in parallel* on bootstrap samples and **averages** them. Targets **variance**: averaging decorrelated models reduces variance, bias unchanged. (Random forests.)
- **Boosting** trains models *sequentially*, each new one focusing on the examples the ensemble still gets wrong (AdaBoost reweights them; gradient boosting fits the residual). Targets **bias**: the ensemble keeps adding capacity aimed at current errors, so a sum of weak (high-bias) learners becomes a strong low-bias model. (XGBoost, LightGBM.)
- **Stacking** trains diverse base models, then trains a **meta-model** on their out-of-fold predictions to learn the best combination. Targets **both**, by letting a learner discover where each base model is trustworthy.

**Why boosting reduces bias and bagging can't:** bagging averages models that all make the *same systematic error* (same bias) — averaging can't remove a shared bias. Boosting instead *adds* new models specifically aimed at the residual error, so it actively reduces bias step by step (at the risk of eventually overfitting, hence learning rate + early stopping). Rule of thumb: high-variance base learner → bag it; high-bias base learner → boost it.
